title: VTE Prevention: Sequential Compression Devices and Antiembolism Stockings
subtitle: The nurse uses mechanical VTE prophylaxis safely — applying SCDs and graduated stockings correctly, checking skin each shift, encouraging early ambulation, and withholding compression for a suspected DVT.

patient.name: VTE Prophylaxis
patient.admitDate: The nurse applies sequential compression devices and antiembolism stockings to prevent venous thromboembolism.

labs.v: Two fingers fit under the sleeve.

nursesNote: The nurse provides mechanical VTE prophylaxis. Immobility causes venous stasis, which raises the risk of deep vein thrombosis; sequential compression devices (SCDs) gently inflate and deflate to push blood back toward the heart, and graduated antiembolism stockings (TED hose) apply pressure that is greatest at the ankle to improve venous return. Stockings are measured for the correct size and applied before the patient rises, with the legs elevated and before edema develops; they are smoothed so there are no wrinkles, since a rolled or bunched stocking acts like a tourniquet. SCD sleeves are kept on continuously except while the patient walks or bathes and are then reapplied. Each shift the nurse removes the device to inspect the skin and check color, warmth, and sensation. Early ambulation, ankle pumps, and hydration are the most effective prevention. Compression is withheld for an active or suspected DVT, severe peripheral arterial disease or leg ischemia, or open skin, and a newly swollen, warm, painful calf is reported without massaging the leg.
providerOrders: Apply sequential compression devices while the patient is on bed rest
Measure for and apply graduated antiembolism stockings before the patient rises
Remove devices each shift to inspect skin and neurovascular status
Encourage early ambulation, ankle pumps, and hydration
Withhold compression and report a suspected DVT or leg ischemia

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about mechanical VTE prevention?

opts.text: SCDs use intermittent compression to promote venous return
Antiembolism stockings are applied before the patient gets out of bed
Devices are removed each shift to inspect the skin
SCDs should stay off most of the shift to rest the legs
Early ambulation is the most effective way to prevent VTE
Stockings may be rolled down at the top for comfort
Venous stasis from immobility raises the risk of clot formation
SCDs and stockings reduce stasis by improving venous return
A wrinkled or rolled stocking can act as a tourniquet
Stockings are best applied after edema develops during the day
Mechanical prophylaxis helps when anticoagulants are contraindicated
Massaging a swollen, painful calf improves circulation
Measure the leg for the correct stocking size
Position the patient supine with legs elevated
Turn the stocking inside out down to the heel
Ease it over the foot and up, smoothing wrinkles
Check the toes for color, warmth, and window alignment
Postop patient on bed rest for VTE prevention
New suspected or confirmed DVT in that leg
Severe peripheral arterial disease or leg ischemia
Removing SCDs while the patient walks, then reapplying
Open wound, dermatitis, or recent skin graft on the leg
Properly measured stockings smoothed without wrinkles
Massaging a red, swollen, painful calf
Encouraging ankle pumps and hydration on bed rest

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: The calf is newly swollen, warm, red, and tender
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Stop compression and do not massage the leg
Assess and compare both legs; check vitals
Notify the provider of suspected DVT
Keep the leg still and maintain bed rest per orders
Document the findings
Applying antiembolism stockings before the patient rises
Leaving SCDs off most of the shift while on bed rest
Removing stockings each shift to inspect the skin
Letting a stocking roll into a tight band at the knee
Encouraging ankle pumps and early ambulation
Applying SCDs to a leg with a new suspected DVT
Smoothing out wrinkles after applying stockings
Massaging a newly swollen, painful calf
